_meta.title: Sequential Logic — Latches, Flip-Flops, FSMs
_meta.note: 4 MCQs on latch vs flip-flop, Mealy vs Moore, state encoding, setup/hold time.

questions.question: What is the key difference between a D latch and a D flip-flop?
options: A D latch uses NOR gates; a D flip-flop uses NAND gates.
A D latch is level-sensitive (transparent when enabled); a D flip-flop captures input only on a clock edge.
A D latch stores multiple bits; a D flip-flop stores only one bit.
A D latch requires a clock; a D flip-flop does not.
questions.explanation: A D latch is level-sensitive — its output follows the input whenever the enable is high. A D flip-flop is edge-triggered, sampling the input only at the rising (or falling) clock edge. This edge-triggered behavior is what makes flip-flops the basis of synchronous design.

questions.question: In a Mealy FSM, the output depends on:
options: The current state only
The current input only
Both the current state and the current input
The previous state and the previous input
questions.explanation: A Mealy machine's output is a function of both the current state and the current input (output = f(state, input)). A Moore machine's output depends on state only. The Mealy model can respond to inputs faster (within the same cycle) but may produce glitches if inputs change mid-cycle.

questions.question: You are designing an FSM with 10 states on an FPGA. Which state encoding is typically fastest for FPGA synthesis, and why?
options: Binary encoding — fewer flip-flops means shorter routing.
Gray encoding — single-bit transitions reduce dynamic power.
One-hot encoding — each state maps to one flip-flop, making next-state decode logic trivial (simple OR/AND gates).
Random encoding — lets the synthesizer explore the full solution space.
questions.explanation: On FPGAs, one-hot encoding is often fastest because each state is a single flip-flop, and the next-state logic reduces to simple multiplexing. The trade-off is using more flip-flops (10 for 10 states vs. 4 for binary), but FPGA fabric has abundant flip-flops. Binary encoding uses fewer flip-flops but creates complex combinational decode logic that increases propagation delay.

questions.question: A flip-flop has a setup time of 0.2 ns and a hold time of 0.1 ns. The clock period is 10 ns. At what time relative to the rising clock edge must the input D become stable to guarantee correct operation?
options: D must be stable at least 0.2 ns before the clock edge and remain stable at least 0.1 ns after it.
D must be stable for the entire 10 ns clock period.
D must be stable at least 0.1 ns before the clock edge and 0.2 ns after it.
D only needs to be stable at the exact moment of the clock edge.
questions.explanation: Setup time (Tsu = 0.2 ns) requires D to be stable BEFORE the clock edge — the input must settle at least 0.2 ns prior. Hold time (Th = 0.1 ns) requires D to remain stable AFTER the edge for at least 0.1 ns. Violating either causes metastability, where the flip-flop output is unpredictable.

questions.question: What distinguishes sequential logic from combinational logic?
options: Sequential logic uses fewer gates
Sequential logic has memory — its outputs depend on current inputs AND previous state
Sequential logic is always faster
Combinational logic can't implement NOT gates
questions.explanation: Combinational logic outputs depend only on current inputs. Sequential logic adds memory elements (flip-flops, latches) so that outputs depend on both current inputs and the stored state. This enables circuits like counters, registers, and finite state machines.
